parts.content: Calculate the speed of the proton in the magnetic field.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $r = \frac{mv}{qB} \Rightarrow v = \frac{qBr}{m}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>


- Substitution: $v = \frac{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.20 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-2}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \approx 5.75 \times 10^5\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the physics principles
steps.content:
This question involves a charged particle moving in a magnetic field, which relates to **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**. 

Since the proton undergoes circular motion, the magnetic force acts as the centripetal force. We will use the **Force Balance Approach** by equating the magnetic force formula to the centripetal force formula.

steps.title: List variables and constants
steps.content:
First, we identify the given values and necessary constants from the data booklet. Note that the radius must be converted to meters (SI units).

**Given values:**
- Magnetic field strength: $B = 0.20\ \text{T}$
- Radius: $r = 3.0\ \text{cm} = 0.030\ \text{m}$

**Constants:**
- Charge of a proton ($e$): $q = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- Mass of a proton ($m_p$): $m = 1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Set up force balance equation
steps.content:
We equate the magnetic force ($F_B$) to the centripetal force ($F_c$).

From the **Fields** section, the magnetic force on a moving charge is $F = qvB\sin\theta$. Since the proton moves perpendicular to the field ($	heta = 90^\circ$), $\sin\theta = 1$, so $F_B = qvB$.

From the **Mechanics** section, the centripetal force is derived from Newton's Second Law ($F=ma$) and centripetal acceleration ($a = \frac{v^2}{r}$), giving $F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}$.

Setting these equal:
$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Rearrange to solve for speed
steps.content:
We need to isolate velocity ($v$). 

$$\begin{aligned} qvB &= \frac{mv^2}{r} \\ qB &= \frac{mv}{r} \quad \text{(divide both sides by } v) \\ qBr &= mv \\ v &= \frac{qBr}{m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Substitute the numerical values into the rearranged equation:

$$v = \frac{(1.60 \times 10^{-19})(0.20)(0.030)}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}}$$ 

Using a calculator, we evaluate this expression.

gdc.instructions: Enter the fraction carefully using brackets or the fraction template.

ti84.instructions: Enter the values using scientific notation (2nd + comma for 'E').

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the speed

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation gives approximately $574,850$. Following the markscheme and significant figures guidelines (rounding to 3 significant figures):

$$v \approx 5.75 \times 10^5\ \text{m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify given values and constants
steps.content:
First, we list the information provided in the question and the required physical constants for a proton found in the data booklet.

**Given:**
- Magnetic field strength: $B = 0.20\ \text{T}$
- Radius: $r = 3.0\ \text{cm} = 0.030\ \text{m}$ (converted to SI units)

**Constants (from Data Booklet):**
- Elementary charge: $e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ (charge of a proton $q$)
- Mass of a proton: $m_p = 1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Derive angular velocity equation
steps.content:
A charged particle moving perpendicular to a magnetic field undergoes circular motion. The magnetic force acts as the centripetal force. 

We use the **Angular Velocity Approach** by equating the magnetic force ($F_B$) to the centripetal force in terms of angular velocity ($F_c = m\omega^2 r$).

Using the relationship between linear and angular velocity $$v = \omega r$$, the magnetic force $$F_B = qvB$$ becomes $$F_B = q(\omega r)B$$.

Equating the forces:
$$\begin{aligned} F_B &= F_c \\ q\omega r B &= m\omega^2 r \\ qB &= m\omega \end{aligned}$$

Solving for angular velocity $\omega$:
$$\omega = \frac{qB}{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate angular velocity
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values to find the angular velocity $\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \frac{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.20}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \\ \omega &\approx 1.916 \times 10^{7}\ \text{rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate angular velocity

steps.title: Calculate linear speed
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the linear speed $v$ using the relation $$v = \omega r$$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= (1.916 \times 10^{7}) \times 0.030 \\ v &\approx 5.75 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme method where $v = \frac{qBr}{m}$.

steps.title: Set up force equation

$$F_B = F_c$$

Using the equations from the **Fields** and **Mechanics** sections:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Identify given values and constants
steps.content:
First, we list the information provided in the question and the relevant physical constants for a **proton** from the data booklet.

From the question:
- Magnetic field strength: $B = 0.20\ \text{T}$
- Radius of path: $r = 3.0\ \text{cm} = 0.030\ \text{m}$ (don't forget to convert to meters)

From the **Physical Constants** in the data booklet:
- Elementary charge: $q = 1.60 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- Mass of proton: $m = 1.67 \times 10^{-27}\ \text{kg}$

steps.title: Set up force equations
steps.content:
For a charged particle moving in a circle due to a magnetic field, the magnetic force acts as the centripetal force. We use the formulas for magnetic force (with $\theta=90^\circ$ so $\sin\theta=1$) and Newton's Second Law for circular motion.

$$qvB = ma = m\frac{v^2}{r}$$

This gives us the relationship between velocity and the magnetic parameters.

steps.title: Derive kinetic energy formula
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Algebraic Substitution** method, we can combine the force equation with the kinetic energy formula to solve for energy without calculating velocity explicitly.

First, rearrange the force equation to solve for $v$:
$$v = \frac{qBr}{m}$$

Now, substitute this expression for $v$ directly into the kinetic energy formula $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_k &= \frac{1}{2}m\left(\frac{qBr}{m}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{1}{2}m\left(\frac{q^2B^2r^2}{m^2}\right) \\ &= \frac{q^2B^2r^2}{2m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Substitute the numerical values into our derived formula:

$$E_k = \frac{(1.60 \times 10^{-19})^2 \times (0.20)^2 \times (0.030)^2}{2 \times (1.67 \times 10^{-27})}$$

Using a calculator to compute:

$$E_k \approx 2.76 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{J}$$

steps.content:
The kinetic energy of the proton is approximately **$2.76 \times 10^{-16}$ J**.

parts.content: Outline one method by which the proton could have been accelerated to this speed.
parts.markscheme: Acceleration through a potential difference using electric fields / accelerating plates / linear accelerator <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the physics principles
steps.content:
First, we must distinguish between the effects of magnetic and electric fields on a charged particle like a proton (covered in **Topic D.3 Motion in electromagnetic fields**).

The question states the proton enters a magnetic field and travels in a "circular path". Recall that the magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity ($$F = qvB\sin\theta$$). This means a magnetic field can change the *direction* of the proton but does **no work** on it, so it cannot increase its speed.

To increase the speed (accelerate) of the proton, we need a force that does work. This requires an **electric field**.

steps.title: Identify the method of acceleration
steps.content:
To accelerate the proton to the required speed, it must experience an electric force acting in the direction of motion. This is achieved by passing the proton through an **electric field** created by a **potential difference** (voltage).

The relationship between the force $F$, electric field strength $E$, and charge $q$ is given by:

$$F = qE$$

Common experimental setups to achieve this include:
*   **Accelerating plates:** Two parallel plates with a high voltage across them.
*   **Linear accelerator (LINAC):** A device that uses oscillating electric fields to accelerate particles in a straight line.
*   **Cyclotron:** A device that uses electric fields to accelerate particles across a gap between two 'D' shaped regions.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
For the single mark, you simply need to outline that an electric field or potential difference is used. 

**Answer:**
The proton is accelerated through a **potential difference** using an **electric field** (or by using a linear accelerator / accelerating plates).

parts.content: Explain why the magnetic field does no work on the proton.
parts.markscheme:
- Force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion <marks> 1 mark </marks>


- No component of force in the direction of motion, hence no work is done (since $W = Fd\cos\theta$ with $\theta = 90^\circ$) <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the Force Direction
steps.content: In physics, when a charged particle like a proton moves through a magnetic field, the magnetic force ($F$) acting on it is always perpendicular to its velocity ($v$). Since velocity represents the instantaneous direction of motion, the force is also perpendicular to the proton's path.

steps.title: Define Displacement and Work
steps.content:
The work done on an object is defined by the formula found in the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

In this equation, $s$ is the displacement and $\theta$ is the angle between the force and the direction of displacement. For a proton moving in a circle, the displacement over an infinitesimal time is in the direction of its velocity.

steps.title: Evaluate the Work Done
steps.content:
Because the magnetic force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion, the angle $\theta$ between the force ($F$) and the displacement ($s$) is exactly $90^\circ$. 

Substituting this into the work formula:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= Fs \cos(90^\circ) \\ W &= Fs \times 0 \\ W &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$, there is no component of the force in the direction of motion, meaning the magnetic field does no work on the proton.

gdc.instructions: Set the angle unit to Degrees in settings, then calculate cos(90).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode, then press the COS key, enter 90, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Switch to Degree mode and type cos(90).

calculator.description: Verification of the cosine of 90 degrees.

steps.title: Identify the force direction
steps.content:
When a charged particle like a proton enters a magnetic field, the magnetic force ($F$) acting on it is always perpendicular to its velocity ($v$). 

We can see this relationship in the magnetic force formula from the data booklet:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

In this case, the force is directed towards the center of the circular path, acting as a centripetal force.

highlights.text: Force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion

steps.title: Analyze the effect on speed
steps.content:
Because the magnetic force acts as a centripetal force (always perpendicular to the motion), it only changes the **direction** of the proton's velocity, not its **magnitude** (speed).

If the speed ($v$) of the proton remains constant, we can look at what happens to its energy. This concept is covered in **Topic A.2: Circular motion**.

steps.title: Apply the Work-Energy Theorem
steps.content:
The kinetic energy of the proton is given by:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

Since the speed ($v$) is constant, the kinetic energy ($E_k$) does not change ($ \Delta E_{k} = 0 $). According to the **Work-Energy Theorem**, the work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy:

$$W = \Delta E_{k}$$

Since there is no change in kinetic energy, the work done by the magnetic field must be zero.

steps.title: Confirm with work formula
steps.content:
We can also confirm this using the definition of work from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos\theta$$

Since the force $F$ is always perpendicular to the displacement $s$, the angle $\theta$ is $90^\circ$. 

Because $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$, the work done is $W = 0$. This earns the second mark by showing that there is no component of force in the direction of motion.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode. Press [COS] [9] [0] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type cos(90) and ensure the angle setting is in degrees.

calculator.description: Verify the value of cosine at 90 degrees.

highlights.text: No component of force in the direction of motion, hence no work is done (since $W = Fd\cos\theta$ with $\theta = 90^\circ$)

parts.content: Suggest why electrons moving with the same speed in the same magnetic field are deflected more than protons.
parts.markscheme: Electron has smaller mass, so $r = \frac{mv}{qB}$ is smaller → greater deflection <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Establish the radius formula
steps.content:
When a charged particle moves perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force acts as a centripetal force, causing it to move in a circle. We can derive the radius of this path by setting the magnetic force equal to the centripetal force:

$$\begin{aligned} F_B &= F_c \\ qvB &= \frac{mv^2}{r} \\ r &= \frac{mv}{qB} \end{aligned}$$

This formula is a key concept in **Topic D.3.2 (Charged particle motion in magnetic fields)**.

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content:
To compare the deflection of the electron and the proton, we look at which variables in the equation $$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$ remain the same according to the question:

- **Speed ($v$):** Both particles are moving with the same speed.
- **Magnetic Field ($B$):** Both are in the same uniform field.
- **Charge magnitude ($q$):** Although they have opposite signs, an electron and a proton have the same magnitude of charge ($e = 1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$).

steps.title: Compare particle masses
steps.content:
The main difference between these two particles is their mass ($m$). 

From our physical constants, we know that an electron has a significantly smaller mass than a proton ($m_e \approx 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$ vs $m_p \approx 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$).

gdc.instructions: Divide the mass of a proton by the mass of an electron.

ti84.instructions: Divide the mass of a proton by the mass of an electron.

desmos.instructions: Divide the mass of a proton by the mass of an electron.

calculator.description: Compare the mass of a proton to an electron to see the scale of the difference.

steps.title: Relate mass to deflection
steps.content:
Since $v$, $q$, and $B$ are constant, the radius is directly proportional to the mass ($r \propto m$). 

Because the electron has a **smaller mass**, it will have a **smaller radius of curvature**. A smaller radius means the path curves more sharply, which results in a greater deflection over the same distance compared to the proton.

steps.title: Compare the magnetic forces
steps.content:
The magnetic force $F$ acting on a charged particle is given by the formula from the **Fields** section of your data booklet:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

In this case, both the proton and the electron have the same magnitude of charge ($e$), are moving at the same speed $v$, and are in the same magnetic field $B$. Therefore, the magnitude of the force acting on both particles is exactly the same.

steps.title: Analyze acceleration using mass
steps.content:
Using Newton's Second Law from the **Mechanics** section:

$$F = ma$$

Rearranging for acceleration, we get $a = \frac{F}{m}$. Since the force $F$ is the same for both particles, the acceleration is inversely proportional to the mass. Because an electron has a much smaller mass than a proton, the electron will experience a much greater acceleration.

steps.title: Relate acceleration to deflection
steps.content:
The acceleration in this circular path is the centripetal acceleration, found in the **Mechanics** section:

$$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

Rearranging for the radius of the path gives $r = \frac{v^2}{a}$. Since both particles have the same speed $v$, the particle with the greater acceleration (the electron) will have a smaller radius of curvature. A smaller radius means a "tighter" turn, which corresponds to more deflection.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content: In summary, because the electron has a much smaller mass than the proton, the same magnetic force causes it to have a much higher acceleration. This higher acceleration results in a smaller orbital radius ($r$), causing the electron to be deflected more significantly than the proton.

parts.content: State the nature of the magnetic force acting on a moving charge.
parts.markscheme:
- Force is always perpendicular to both the magnetic field and velocity vector of the particle <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the relevant physics
steps.content: When a charged particle, such as an electron, moves through a uniform magnetic field, it experiences a magnetic force ($F$). This interaction is a fundamental concept in **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**.

steps.title: Analyze the force formula
steps.content:
The magnitude of this force is provided in your IB Physics data booklet under the **Fields** section:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

In this equation, $q$ is the charge, $v$ is the velocity, $B$ is the magnetic field strength, and $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity and the magnetic field. This formula helps us understand how the magnitude changes, but it also implies a specific directional relationship between these vectors.

steps.title: Define the vector relationship
steps.content:
Using the **Vector Directional Relationship** approach, we define the direction of the magnetic force. The magnetic force is always a deflecting force that acts in a direction **perpendicular** to both the velocity vector ($v$) of the charge and the magnetic field vector ($B$).

This means the force is always at a $90^{\circ}$ angle to the direction the electron is currently moving, which is why it causes the circular path described in the question.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: To earn the mark according to the markscheme, you must state that the magnetic force is always perpendicular to both the magnetic field and the velocity vector of the particle.

steps.title: Recognize circular motion
steps.content: The question states that the electrons follow a **semicircular path**. In physics, any object moving in a circular arc requires a **centripetal force**—a force that always points towards the center of the circle. This concept is covered in **Topic A.2 (Circular motion)**.

steps.title: Analyze the force direction
steps.content:
Using the formula for magnetic force on a moving charge from the data booklet:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

When a magnetic field is applied perpendicular to the velocity, the resulting force acts as a **deflecting force**. This force is always **perpendicular** to the direction of the velocity (the instantaneous motion) and also perpendicular to the magnetic field lines.

steps.title: State the final nature
steps.content:
Because the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, it changes the direction of the electron beam without changing its speed. 

To earn the mark, you must state that the nature of the force is that it is **always perpendicular to both the magnetic field and the velocity vector** of the particle.

parts.content: Describe the motion of an electron in a magnetic field when it enters at right angles to the field lines.
parts.markscheme:
- Electron moves in a circular path in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field <marks>A1</marks>

- Magnetic force continuously changes the direction of velocity, providing centripetal force <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the magnetic force
steps.content:
When a charged particle like an electron moves through a magnetic field, it experiences a magnetic force. According to the **Fields** section of your data booklet, this force $F$ is given by:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

Since the electron enters at **right angles** to the field lines, $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$. The magnitude of the force is constant as long as the speed $v$ and magnetic field $B$ remain constant.

highlights.text: at right angles to the field lines

steps.title: Determine force direction
steps.content:
The magnetic force is always perpendicular to both the magnetic field lines and the velocity of the electron (you can find this direction using Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, remembering that an electron has a negative charge).

Because the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, it cannot change the electron's speed—it only changes the **direction** of the velocity.

steps.title: Relate to centripetal force
steps.content:
In physics, a force that is constant in magnitude and always perpendicular to the direction of motion acts as a **centripetal force**. This is a concept from **Topic A.2.4 Circular motion**.

From the **Mechanics** section, we know the centripetal acceleration is:

$$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

The magnetic force provides this acceleration, pulling the electron into a curve.

steps.title: Describe the final motion
steps.content:
Because the magnetic force continuously changes the direction of the velocity while acting as a centripetal force, the electron follows a **circular path** (or a semicircular path if it leaves the field) in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field.

To earn full marks, ensure you mention:
1. The path is **circular**.
2. The magnetic force acts as a **centripetal force** by changing the direction of velocity.

steps.title: Identify the Magnetic Force
steps.content:
When a charged particle like an electron enters a magnetic field, it experiences a magnetic force ($F$). According to the formula in **Topic D: Fields**, the magnitude of this force is:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

Since the question states the electron enters at **right angles** to the field lines, $\theta = 90^\circ$. Because $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$, the force is simply $F = qvB$. Crucially, this force is always perpendicular to both the magnetic field and the electron's velocity.

steps.title: Calculate Work Done
steps.content:
Let's look at this from a work-energy perspective using the work formula from **Topic A: Mechanics**:

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

In this scenario, the magnetic force ($F$) is always perpendicular to the displacement ($s$) of the electron at every instant. This means the angle between the force and the direction of motion is $90^\circ$.

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to Degrees. Enter cos(90).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode, then press COS followed by 90.

desmos.instructions: Type 'cos(90)' and ensure the angle setting is set to Degrees.

calculator.description: Verify the value of cos(90°) to determine the work done.

steps.title: Analyze Energy and Speed
steps.content:
Since $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$, the work done ($W$) by the magnetic field on the electron is **zero**. 

According to the Work-Energy Theorem, the change in kinetic energy is equal to the work done. If $W = 0$, then the kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$) remains constant. 

This tells us a vital piece of information: the **speed of the electron does not change**; only its direction is affected by the force.

steps.title: Describe the Resulting Path
steps.content:
Because the force is constant in magnitude ($F = qvB$) and always acts perpendicular to the velocity, it acts as a **centripetal force**. 

From **Topic A.2 (Circular Motion)**, we know that a force acting towards a fixed center causes the particle to move in a **circular path**. 

$$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$

To earn the marks on the exam, you must specify that the electron moves in a circle in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field lines.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
In summary, the motion is described by these two key points:
1. The electron follows a **circular path** (in a plane perpendicular to the field).
2. The magnetic force acts as a **centripetal force**, continuously changing the electron's direction while its speed remains constant.

highlights.text: Electron moves in a circular path in a plane perpendicular to the magnetic field
Magnetic force continuously changes the direction of velocity, providing centripetal force

parts.content: Calculate the time taken for an electron moving at $1.5 \times 10^7\ \text{m s}^{-1}$ to complete a semicircular path of radius $5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m.
parts.markscheme:
- Time for full circle $T = \frac{2\pi r}{v}$ so time for half circle $t = \frac{\pi r}{v}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $t = \frac{\pi \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^7} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ s <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept answers in the range $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ s to $1.1 \times 10^{-8}$ s.</note>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the time taken, we first identify the known values from the question:

*   Speed of the electron ($v$) = $1.5 \times 10^7\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
*   Radius of the path ($r$) = $5.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}$
*   Path shape: Semicircle

steps.title: Calculate the arc length
steps.content:
The distance the electron travels ($s$) is the length of the semicircular path. Since the circumference of a full circle is $2\pi r$, the distance for a semicircle is half of that:

$$s = \pi r$$

Substituting the given radius:

$$s = \pi \times (5.0 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m})$$

gdc.instructions: Enter π * 5.0 * 10^(-2) and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter π * 5.0 * 10^(-2) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type pi * 5.0 * 10^-2 into the calculation bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the distance of the semicircle.

steps.title: Determine the time taken
steps.content:
Using the relationship between speed, distance, and time from **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)**, we know that for constant speed:

$$v = \frac{s}{t} \implies t = \frac{s}{v}$$

Substituting our expression for arc length ($s = \pi r$):

$$t = \frac{\pi r}{v}$$

Now, substitute the numerical values:

$$t = \frac{\pi \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2}}{1.5 \times 10^7}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter (π * 5.0 * 10^(-2)) / (1.5 * 10^7) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (pi * 5.0 * 10^-2) / (1.5 * 10^7) into the calculation bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the time in seconds.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
From the calculation:

$$t \approx 1.047 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (matching the given values in the question):

$$t = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{s}$$

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
To calculate the time, we first identify the velocity $v$ and the radius $r$ of the electron's path from the question:

- Speed $v = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- Radius $r = 5.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate full orbital period
steps.content:
The time taken for an object to complete one full circular revolution is called the period $T$. Using the relationship for circular motion (Topic A.2.4), we know that speed is distance divided by time. For a full circle, the distance is the circumference $2\pi r$:

$$v = \frac{2\pi r}{T}$$

Rearranging for the period $T$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi r}{v}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time for one full revolution.

steps.title: Find semicircular travel time
steps.content:
Since the electron follows a **semicircular** path, it only travels half of a full circle. Therefore, the time taken $t$ is exactly half of the orbital period $T$:

$$t = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{\pi r}{v}$$

Now, substitute the values:

$$t = \frac{\pi \times (5.0 \times 10^{-2})}{1.5 \times 10^7}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the time for the semicircular path.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives:

$$t \approx 1.047 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}$$

Rounding to two significant figures, as given in the problem values, we get:

$$t = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}$$

steps.title: Identify given parameters
steps.content:
First, let's identify the information provided in the question. We are given the speed of the electron and the radius of the circular path it follows.

*   Velocity ($v$) = $1.5 \times 10^7\ \text{m s}^{-1}$
*   Radius ($r$) = $5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m

This problem involves circular motion, which you can find in **Topic A.2.4 Circular motion** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Determine angular velocity
steps.content:
Using the angular velocity approach, we first find how fast the electron is rotating. The relationship between linear velocity ($v$) and angular velocity ($\omega$) is:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} \omega &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{5.0 \times 10^{-2}} \\ \omega &= 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ rad s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 1.5e7 by 5.0e-2.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1.5 * 10^7) / (5.0 * 10^-2) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular velocity omega.

steps.title: Identify angular displacement
steps.content:
The question specifies that the electron follows a **semicircular path**. 

A full circle corresponds to an angular displacement of $2\pi$ radians. Therefore, a semicircle corresponds to exactly half of that:

$$\theta = \pi \text{ radians}$$

steps.title: Calculate time taken
steps.content:
Finally, we use the definition of angular velocity, which is angular displacement divided by time ($\omega = \frac{\theta}{t}$), to solve for time ($t$):

$$t = \frac{\theta}{\omega}$$

Substituting our values for $\theta$ and $\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned} t &= \frac{\pi}{3.0 \times 10^8} \\ t &\approx 1.047 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to two significant figures, we get:

$$t = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide pi by 3.0e8.

ti84.instructions: Enter π / (3.0 * 10^8) and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the time taken for the semicircular path.

parts.content: Determine the magnetic field strength if the radius of the path is $5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m and the speed of the electron is as given in Part 3.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $r = \frac{mv}{qB} \Rightarrow B = \frac{mv}{qr}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitution: $B = \frac{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.5 \times 10^7}{1.60 \times 10^{-19} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $B \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-3}$ T <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the circular motion forces
steps.content:
When a charged particle like an electron moves perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field, it experiences a constant magnetic force that acts as a centripetal force, causing it to follow a circular path. This concept is covered in **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**.

From the **Fields** section of your data booklet, the magnetic force is:
$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

Since the field is perpendicular to the motion, $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$, so the force is simply $F = qvB$.

highlights.text: perpendicular to the direction of motion

steps.title: Set up the force balance
steps.content:
To maintain circular motion, this magnetic force must equal the centripetal force. From the **Mechanics** section, centripetal acceleration is $a = \frac{v^2}{r}$, and using Newton's Second Law ($F=ma$):

$$F_{cent} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

Equating the two forces gives us the centripetal force balance:
$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Solve for magnetic field strength
steps.content:
We need to find the magnetic field strength $B$. We can rearrange the force balance equation by dividing both sides by $qv$:

$$\begin{aligned} B &= \frac{mv^2}{qvr} \\ B &= \frac{mv}{qr} \end{aligned}$$

This formula allows us to calculate $B$ using the properties of the electron and the path described in the question.

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into our equation:
- Mass of an electron ($m$): $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg (from constants)
- Speed of the electron ($v$): $1.5 \times 10^7$ m/s
- Charge of an electron ($q$): $1.60 \times 10^{-19}$ C (from constants)
- Radius of path ($r$): $5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m

$$B = \frac{(9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (1.5 \times 10^7)}{(1.60 \times 10^{-19}) \times (5.0 \times 10^{-2})}$$

$$B \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the division key and parentheses to ensure the numerator and denominator are grouped correctly.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen using the 'EE' key (2nd + ',') for scientific notation.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into a cell. Desmos handles scientific notation using '10^'.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnetic field strength $B$.

highlights.text: radius of the path is $5.0 \times 10^{-2}$ m

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the known values for the electron from the question and the data booklet:

*   Speed of electron ($v$): $1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
*   Radius of path ($r$): $5.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$
*   Charge of an electron ($q$): $1.60 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
*   Mass of an electron ($m$): $9.11 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$

highlights.text: radius of the path is 5.0 \times 10^{-2} m

steps.title: Calculate angular velocity
steps.content:
The angular velocity ($\omega$) is the rate at which the electron rotates around the center of its circular path. We can find this using the relationship:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\omega = \frac{1.5 \times 10^7}{5.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Input 1.5e7 divided by 5.0e-2.

ti84.instructions: Enter (1.5e7) / (5.0e-2) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1.5*10^7 / 5.0*10^-2 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the angular velocity (omega).

steps.title: Derive magnetic field formula
steps.content:
The magnetic force provides the centripetal force required for circular motion. 

$$\begin{aligned} F_B &= F_c \\ qvB &= m\omega^2r \end{aligned}$$

Since $v = \omega r$, we can substitute this into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} q(\omega r)B &= m\omega^2r \\ qB &= m\omega \\ B &= \frac{m\omega}{q} \end{aligned}$$

This formula allows us to find the magnetic field directly from the angular velocity.

steps.title: Solve for magnetic field
steps.content:
Now we substitute the mass of the electron, the angular velocity we calculated, and the elementary charge into our derived formula:

$$B = \frac{9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 3.0 \times 10^8}{1.60 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$B \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$$

Rounding to two significant figures, we get a magnetic field strength of $1.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$.

gdc.instructions: Input (9.11e-31 * 3.0e8) divided by 1.60e-19.

ti84.instructions: Enter (9.11e-31 * 3.0e8) / 1.60e-19 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnetic field strength B.

parts.content: Suggest why a semicircular path is preferable in this setup compared to a full circular path.
parts.markscheme:
- Semicircular path focuses beam toward screen without electrons recirculating or escaping detection area <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the device layout
steps.content: In a cathode ray tube (CRT), the goal is to direct a beam of electrons from a source (the cathode) onto a **fluorescent screen** where they can be detected as a point of light. The physical layout usually places the screen at a specific position relative to the electron source.

steps.title: Consider the full circle
steps.content:
If the electrons followed a full circular path ($360^{\circ}$), they would eventually return to their starting point—the electron gun. 

In this scenario, the electrons would **recirculate** back toward the source rather than reaching the detector (the screen). This would make it impossible to measure the beam's properties or create an image on the screen.

steps.title: Evaluate the semicircular path
steps.content:
By designing the setup so that the electrons follow a **semicircular path**, the beam is diverted $180^{\circ}$ away from its original path. 

This ensures that the electrons are directed toward the screen (the detection area) and stay within the range where they can be measured, without escaping the device or hitting the source casing.

steps.title: Final Justification
steps.content: To earn the mark, you must explain that the semicircular path correctly directs the beam toward the screen while preventing the electrons from **recirculating** or missing the detection area.

steps.title: Identify beam divergence
steps.content: In a real cathode ray tube, the electron beam is never perfectly narrow. Due to the way electrons are emitted, they enter the magnetic field with a slight spread of initial angles, known as **divergence**. For the device to work effectively, these diverging electrons need to be brought back together to create a sharp image.

steps.title: Explain the focusing property
steps.content:
A 180-degree deflection (a semicircle) has a unique geometric property: it naturally **focuses** the beam. Electrons that enter the field at slightly different angles will follow slightly different circular paths, but the geometry of these paths causes them to converge (meet) at the same point after completing exactly half a circle.

This ensures that the impact point on the fluorescent screen is sharp and well-defined, rather than being a blurry spread of electrons.

steps.title: Compare with full circles
steps.content: If the setup allowed for a full circular path, the electrons would simply return to the starting source or continue to orbit within the field. By using a semicircle, we ensure the electrons hit the screen at the exact moment they are most focused, and we prevent them from **recirculating** or escaping the detection area.

highlights.text: Semicircular path focuses beam toward screen without electrons recirculating or escaping detection area

steps.title: Final recommendation
steps.content: To earn the mark, you should mention that the semicircular path is used to **focus the beam** onto the screen and avoid issues like **recirculation**. This property is a key application of Topic D.3 (Motion in electromagnetic fields) in particle detectors and mass spectrometers.

parts.content: State the direction of the magnetic force acting on the particle at the position shown.
parts.markscheme:
Towards the center of the circle

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the type of motion
steps.content: The question specifies that the charged particle is undergoing circular motion. This is a crucial clue for determining the direction of the force without needing complex hand rules.

steps.title: Apply Circular Motion Principles
steps.content:
According to the principles of **Topic A.2.4 Circular motion**, for any particle to maintain a circular path, there must be a net force acting on it directed towards the center of the circle. This net force is known as the **centripetal force**.

From Newton's Second Law ($$F=ma$$) and the centripetal acceleration formula ($$a=\frac{v^{2}}{r}$$), the force causing the acceleration must point in the same direction as the acceleration—towards the center.

steps.title: Determine force direction
steps.content:
In this scenario, the magnetic force is the only force acting perpendicular to the particle's velocity. Therefore, the magnetic force acts as the centripetal force.

Since the centripetal force is always directed towards the center of the rotation, the magnetic force acting on the particle must also be directed **towards the center of the circle**.

steps.title: Identify the relevant rule
steps.content:
To find the direction of the force on a moving charge in a magnetic field, we use **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**. This rule applies to positive charges (or conventional current).

Since the problem states the particle is **positively charged**, we can apply the rule directly without reversing the direction.

steps.title: Assign fingers to vectors
steps.content:
Prepare your left hand by positioning your thumb, first finger, and second finger at right angles to each other:

*   **First Finger**: Magnetic Field ($B$)
*   **Second Finger**: Current or Velocity ($v$) for a positive charge
*   **Thumb**: Force ($F$)

steps.title: Align with magnetic field
steps.content:
The question states the magnetic field is **directed out of the page**. 

Point your **First Finger** directly towards your face (away from the screen/paper).

steps.title: Align with velocity
steps.content: The particle is moving in a circular path. At any point, its velocity is tangent to the circle. Point your **Second Finger** in the direction of the particle's motion at the specific position shown in the diagram.

steps.title: Determine force direction
steps.content:
With your First Finger pointing towards you and your Second Finger aligned with the velocity, look at your **Thumb**. Your Thumb points perpendicular to the velocity, directed inward.

Since the particle is maintaining a circular path, the magnetic force acts as the centripetal force, which is always directed towards the center of rotation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The magnetic force is directed **towards the center of the circle**.

parts.content: Outline why the particle moves in a circular path in this magnetic field.
parts.markscheme:
- The magnetic force is perpendicular to the velocity of the particle <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- This constant perpendicular force causes centripetal acceleration, resulting in circular motion <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the force direction
steps.content:
First, we consider the direction of the magnetic force acting on the charged particle. A key property of the magnetic force is that it always acts **perpendicular** to the velocity vector of the particle.

This relationship is defined by the cross-product nature of the magnetic force, where the force direction is determined by the Right-Hand Rule (or Left-Hand Rule) applied to the velocity and magnetic field vectors.

steps.title: Identify the centripetal force
steps.content:
Since the force is always perpendicular to the motion, it does no work on the particle and does not change its speed. Instead, it only changes the direction of motion.

A force that is constant in magnitude and always directed perpendicular to velocity towards a central point is the definition of a **centripetal force**. This force provides the centripetal acceleration ($$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$) required to sustain circular motion.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Therefore, the particle moves in a circular path because the magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force perpendicular to its velocity.

parts.content: State what would happen to the radius of the path if the speed of the particle were increased.
parts.markscheme:
The radius of the circular path would increase

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the forces
steps.content:
To determine how the radius changes, we need to understand the forces acting on the particle. Since the particle is moving in a circular path, the magnetic force acts as the centripetal force.

This concept is covered in **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**.

steps.title: Expression for magnetic force
steps.content:
First, let's find the expression for the magnetic force. From the **Fields** section of the data booklet, we use the formula for the force on a moving charge:

$$F=qvB\sin\theta$$

Since the particle moves in a circular path, its velocity is perpendicular to the magnetic field, so $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$. This simplifies the force to:

$$F_B = qvB$$

steps.title: Expression for centripetal force
steps.content:
Next, we express the centripetal force required to keep the particle in circular motion. From the **Mechanics** section, we combine Newton's second law ($F=ma$) with the formula for centripetal acceleration ($a = v^2/r$):

$$F_c = m\frac{v^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Equate the forces
steps.content:
Since the magnetic force *is* the centripetal force, we can equate the two expressions:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Solve for radius
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for the radius ($r$). By multiplying both sides by $r$ and dividing by $qvB$, we derive the relationship:

$$\begin{aligned} r \cdot qvB &= mv^2 \\ r &= \frac{mv^2}{qvB} \\ r &= \frac{mv}{qB} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the effect of speed
steps.content:
Looking at the derived equation $$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$, we can see that the radius ($r$) is directly proportional to the speed ($v$).

Therefore, if the speed of the particle is increased, the **radius of the path would increase**.

highlights.text: speed of the particle were increased

steps.title: Recall the radius formula
steps.content:
To understand how the radius changes, we need the formula relating the radius of the path to the speed of the particle. For a charged particle moving perpendicular to a magnetic field, the magnetic force provides the centripetal force.

From the data booklet, we have the magnetic force $$F = qvB\sin\theta$$ (where $$\theta=90^\circ$$) and Newton's second law $$F = ma$$. Using the centripetal acceleration formula $$a = \frac{v^2}{r}$$:

$$qvB = m\frac{v^2}{r}$$

Rearranging this gives the standard radius formula:
$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

steps.title: Analyze proportionality
steps.content:
Now we examine the relationship between the radius and speed using the derived formula:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

In this situation, the mass ($$m$$), charge ($$q$$), and magnetic field strength ($$B$$) are all constant. This means the radius is directly proportional to the speed:

$$r \propto v$$

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Since the radius is directly proportional to the speed, if the speed of the particle is increased, the radius of the circular path must also **increase**.

parts.content: Identify one other factor (besides speed) that affects the radius of the particle's circular path.
parts.markscheme:
Any one of:

- The magnetic field strength
- The charge of the particle
- The mass of the particle

<marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant forces
steps.content:
To find what affects the radius, we first need to understand the forces causing the circular motion. The magnetic force provides the centripetal force required to keep the particle moving in a circle.

From the **Fields** section of the data booklet, the magnetic force on a moving charge is:

$$F=qvB\sin\theta$$

From the **Mechanics** section, centripetal force is given by Newton's second law ($F=ma$) combined with centripetal acceleration ($a = \frac{v^2}{r}$):

$$F = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Equate the forces
steps.content:
Since the particle is moving in a circle, the velocity is perpendicular to the magnetic field, so $\theta = 90^\circ$ and $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$. We can equate the magnitude of the magnetic force to the centripetal force:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Derive the radius formula
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for the radius $r$. We can multiply both sides by $r$ and divide by $qvB$:

$$\begin{aligned} r \cdot qvB &= mv^2 \\ r &= \frac{mv^2}{qvB} \\ r &= \frac{mv}{qB} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the factors
steps.content:
Looking at our derived formula $$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$, we can see which variables affect the radius $r$:

*   $m$: **Mass** of the particle
*   $v$: **Speed** of the particle (already mentioned in the question)
*   $q$: **Charge** of the particle
*   $B$: **Magnetic field strength**

The question asks for one factor *besides* speed.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
You can state any of the variables identified in the previous step (other than speed). Common answers include:

*   **The magnetic field strength ($B$)**
*   **The charge of the particle ($q$)**
*   **The mass of the particle ($m$)**

steps.title: Analyze the motion conceptually
steps.content:
To determine what affects the radius, we can view the circular motion as a competition between two physical principles:

1.  **Inertia**: The particle's tendency to continue moving in a straight line.
2.  **Deflecting Force**: The magnetic force that pulls the particle inward, bending its path into a circle.

steps.title: Consider the effect of mass
steps.content:
First, consider the **mass** ($m$) of the particle. Mass is a measure of inertia. A particle with greater mass is harder to turn; it resists the deflecting force more strongly. 

Therefore, a heavier particle requires more distance to turn, resulting in a **larger radius**.

steps.title: Consider the deflecting force
steps.content:
Next, consider the strength of the deflecting force. A stronger inward force will bend the particle's path more sharply, resulting in a **smaller radius**.

The magnetic force is given by the equation $$F = qvB$$ (assuming velocity is perpendicular to the field).

This tells us two other factors affect the force:
*   **Charge ($q$)**: A particle with more charge experiences a stronger magnetic force.
*   **Magnetic field strength ($B$)**: A stronger magnetic field exerts a stronger force.

steps.title: State the valid factors
steps.content:
Based on this reasoning, the other factors besides speed that determine the radius are:

*   The **mass** of the particle (affects inertia)
*   The **charge** of the particle (affects force)
*   The **magnetic field strength** (affects force)

You only need to list one of these.

questions.specification:
The diagram shows a proton entering a uniform electric field between two oppositely charged parallel plates. The electric field is directed vertically downward, and the proton enters with an initial velocity $v$ at an angle of $35^\circ$ above the horizontal.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/uALA1bpyQGNP3EoLViTm7.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic D - Fields > D.3 Motion in electromagnetic fields > D.3.1 Charged particle motion in electric fields
labeledSubSubTopics.title: D.3.1 Charged particle motion in electric fields
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The core of the question involves the motion of a proton (charged particle) through a uniform electric field between parallel plates, specifically analyzing the force and the resulting trajectory.
