parts.content: State the equation for the force on a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field and explain the factors that influence the magnitude of this force.
parts.markscheme:
- The force on a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by:
$$
F=q v B \sin \theta
$$
where $q$ is the charge, $v$ is the velocity, $B$ is the magnetic field strength, and $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field. <marks>A1</marks>

- The magnitude of the force is greatest when $\theta=90^{\circ}$ (perpendicular motion) and zero when $\theta=0^{\circ}$ (parallel motion). <marks>B1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State the governing equation
steps.content:
To find the force on a charged particle moving through a magnetic field, we look at the **Fields** section of the IB Physics data booklet. The fundamental equation is:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

This formula allows us to calculate the magnitude of the magnetic force ($F$) acting on a charge ($q$) moving with a specific velocity ($v$) through a magnetic field of strength ($B$).

steps.title: Define the variables
steps.content:
In IB Physics, it is crucial to clearly define each term in the equation to ensure you're applying it correctly:

*   $F$: Magnetic force (measured in Newtons, $\text{N}$)
*   $q$: Magnitude of the electric charge (measured in Coulombs, $\text{C}$)
*   $v$: Speed of the particle (measured in $\text{m s}^{-1}$)
*   $B$: Magnetic field strength (measured in Tesla, $\text{T}$)
*   $\theta$: The angle between the velocity vector ($v$) and the magnetic field lines ($B$)

steps.title: Analyze proportional factors
steps.content:
Using **Variable Analysis**, we can see that the magnitude of the force is directly proportional to three main factors:

1.  **Charge ($q$):** A larger charge experiences a greater force. Doubling the charge doubles the force.
2.  **Velocity ($v$):** The faster the particle moves, the stronger the interaction with the field. If the particle is stationary ($v=0$), the magnetic force is zero.
3.  **Magnetic Field Strength ($B$):** A stronger external field results in a larger force on the charge.

steps.title: Analyze the angular influence
steps.content:
The $\sin\theta$ term is critical because it tells us that the relative direction of motion matters just as much as speed:

*   **Maximum Force:** When the particle moves **perpendicular** to the field ($\theta = 90^{\circ}$), $\sin(90^{\circ}) = 1$. The force is at its maximum: $F = qvB$.
*   **Zero Force:** When the particle moves **parallel** to the field ($\theta = 0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$), $\sin(0^{\circ}) = 0$. No magnetic force is exerted, regardless of how fast the particle is moving or how strong the field is.

highlights.text: The magnitude of the force is greatest when $\theta=90^{\circ}$ (perpendicular motion) and zero when $\theta=0^{\circ}$ (parallel motion).

parts.content: Describe the path followed by a charged particle that enters a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly to the field lines, and explain why this path is observed.
parts.markscheme:
- The charged particle follows a circular path when it enters the field perpendicularly. <marks>A1</marks>

- This occurs because the magnetic force acts as a centripetal force, always perpendicular to the velocity, causing the particle to continuously change direction without changing speed. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the path
steps.content:
When a charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly to the field lines, the shape of its trajectory is a **circle** (or a circular arc).

This is a standard motion covered in **Topic D.3: Motion in electromagnetic fields**. If the particle entered at an angle other than $90^\circ$, the path would be a helix, but since it enters perpendicularly, it stays in a single plane moving in a circle.

steps.title: Determine the magnetic force
steps.content:
The magnitude of the force acting on the charge is given by the formula for magnetic force on a moving charge from your data booklet:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

Because the particle enters **perpendicularly**, the angle $\theta$ between the velocity $v$ and the magnetic field $B$ is $90^\circ$. Since $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$, the force is constant at:

$$F = qvB$$

steps.title: Analyze the force direction
steps.content:
Using the right-hand rule (or Fleming's left-hand rule for conventional current), we find that the magnetic force is always **perpendicular** to the velocity vector of the particle.

Because the force is always at right angles to the motion:
1. It does no work on the particle.
2. The speed of the particle remains constant.
3. Only the **direction** of the velocity changes.

steps.title: Connect to centripetal motion
steps.content:
In **Topic A.2: Circular motion**, we learned that any resultant force that acts perpendicularly to the velocity of an object is a **centripetal force**. 

In this case, the magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force to keep the particle in a circular path. This constant change in direction at a constant speed results in uniform circular motion, where the magnetic force equals the centripetal force:

$$qvB = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

This explains why the observed path is circular.

steps.title: Identify the path
steps.content: When a charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly, it follows a **circular path**.

highlights.text: The charged particle follows a circular path when it enters the field perpendicularly.

steps.title: Determine the magnetic force
steps.content:
From the data booklet, the magnetic force $F$ on a charge $q$ moving with velocity $v$ in a magnetic field $B$ is given by:

$$F = qvB \sin\theta$$

Because the particle enters **perpendicularly**, the angle $\theta$ is $90^\circ$. Since $\sin(90^\circ) = 1$, the force is:

$$F = qvB$$

steps.title: Relate to centripetal force
steps.content:
Using Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (or the right-hand palm rule), we know this force is always perpendicular to the direction of motion (velocity). In physics, a constant force that is always perpendicular to velocity acts as a **centripetal force**, as studied in **Topic A.2 (Circular motion)**.

From the data booklet, centripetal acceleration is $a = \frac{v^2}{r}$. Applying Newton's Second Law ($F=ma$):

$$F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

steps.title: Justify the circular motion
steps.content:
By equating the magnetic force to the centripetal force, we can analyze the motion:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{magnetic} &= F_{centripetal} \\ qvB &= \frac{mv^2}{r} \end{aligned}$$

Solving for the radius $r$:

$$r = \frac{mv}{qB}$$

Since the mass $m$, charge $q$, velocity $v$, and magnetic field $B$ are all constant, the **radius $r$ remains constant**. A path with a constant radius is a circle.

highlights.text: This occurs because the magnetic force acts as a centripetal force, always perpendicular to the velocity, causing the particle to continuously change direction without changing speed.

steps.title: Identify the path
steps.content:
When a charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly to the field lines, it follows a **circular path**.

This is the first mark in the markscheme. To understand why this happens, we need to look at the relationship between force, work, and energy.

highlights.text: enters a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly to the field lines

steps.title: Analyze work done
steps.content:
The magnetic force ($F$) acting on the charge is given by the formula:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

Crucially, according to the right-hand rule (or left-hand rule for negative charges), this force is **always perpendicular** to the velocity ($v$) and therefore perpendicular to the instantaneous displacement ($s$). 

We can calculate the work done ($W$) using the formula from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

Since the force is perpendicular to the displacement, the angle $\theta$ between them is $90^\circ$. Because $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$, the work done by the magnetic force is always **zero**.

steps.title: Relate work to speed
steps.content:
According to the work-energy theorem, the net work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy:

$$W = \Delta E_k$$

Since $W = 0$, there is no change in kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$). This means the **speed** of the particle remains constant throughout its motion.

steps.title: Explain circular motion
steps.content:
Even though the speed is constant, the magnetic force is still acting on the particle. Because the force direction is always perpendicular to the velocity, it constantly 'steers' the particle by changing its direction without changing its speed.

This is the definition of **centripetal force**. A constant magnitude force that is always perpendicular to the velocity results in a **circular path** with a constant radius.

highlights.text: magnetic force acts as a centripetal force

parts.content: Explain how the angle of the particle's velocity with respect to the magnetic field affects the force experienced by the particle.
parts.markscheme:
The magnetic force is given by the relationship $F = qvB \sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field vector. <marks>M1</marks>

- When $\theta = 0^\circ$ (velocity is parallel to the field), $\sin\theta = 0$, so the magnetic force is zero. <marks>A1</marks>

- When $\theta = 90^\circ$ (velocity is perpendicular to the field), $\sin\theta = 1$, so the magnetic force is at its maximum. <marks>A1</marks>

For angles between $0^\circ$ and $90^\circ$, the force increases as the angle increases according to the sine function.

parts.content: Describe the path of the particle in the magnetic field.
parts.markscheme:
- The particle will follow a helical path. <marks>A1</marks>

- The component of velocity parallel to the magnetic field remains constant, resulting in uniform linear motion along the direction of the field. <marks>A1</marks>

- The component of velocity perpendicular to the magnetic field results in uniform circular motion. <marks>A1</marks>

<note>The radius of the helix $r = \frac{mv\sin\theta}{qB}$ remains constant as $m, v, \theta, q,$ and $B$ are all constant. The particle's speed remains constant because the magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity vector, meaning no work is done on the particle.</note>

<examTip>Draw a clear diagram showing the helical path and label the relevant vectors (velocity, magnetic field, and force).</examTip>

steps.title: Decompose the initial velocity
steps.content:
To understand the motion, we split the particle's velocity $v$ into two components relative to the magnetic field $B$. This is a key technique in **Topic D.3 (Motion in electromagnetic fields)**.

1.  **Perpendicular component ($v_{\perp}$):** The part of the velocity that is at a right angle to the field lines.
2.  **Parallel component ($v_{\parallel}$):** The part of the velocity that is directed along the field lines.

Using trigonometry:
$$\begin{aligned} v_{\perp} &= v \sin(60^\circ) \approx 3.46 \times 10^5 \text{ m/s} \\ v_{\parallel} &= v \cos(60^\circ) = 2.00 \times 10^5 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the velocity components.

highlights.text: at an angle of 60^∘ to the field

steps.title: Analyze the circular motion
steps.content:
The perpendicular component ($v_{\perp}$) experiences a magnetic force. According to the formula for magnetic force on a charge:

$$F = qvB\sin\theta$$

Because this force is always perpendicular to $v_{\perp}$, it acts as a centripetal force. This results in **uniform circular motion** in the plane perpendicular to the magnetic field. Since the force does no work (it is always perpendicular to motion), the speed in this plane remains constant.

steps.title: Analyze the linear motion
steps.content:
Now consider the parallel component ($v_{\parallel}$). In the formula $F = qvB\sin\theta$, if the motion is parallel to the field, the angle $\theta$ is $0^\circ$.

Since $\sin(0^\circ) = 0$, there is no magnetic force acting in the direction of the field lines. Therefore, according to Newton’s First Law, the particle maintains **constant velocity** along the field lines, resulting in uniform linear motion.

steps.title: Describe the helical path
steps.content:
The actual path is a combination of these two simultaneous motions:
1. Circular motion around the field lines.
2. Constant linear motion along the field lines.

When combined, the particle follows a **helical path** (like a corkscrew). 

Marks are awarded for:
- Identifying the path as **helical**.
- Explaining that the **parallel velocity component** leads to uniform linear motion.
- Explaining that the **perpendicular velocity component** leads to uniform circular motion.
